content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 27, 2017

Amy Albano
City Attorney
City of Burbank
275 East Olive Avenue
Burbank, CA 91510-6459

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-17-208

Dear Ms. Albano:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

     Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. Is a councilmember who owns a residence within 500 feet of a proposed housing project disqualified from taking part in the decisions regarding the project?

2. If disqualified, to what extent can the councilmember comment on the project as a member of the public at meeting attended by other city officials, at public meetings not attended by other city officials, and with the press?

3. If disqualified from decisions regarding the project, can the councilmember comment on the scope of any required or draft environmental impact report?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-208
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. The councilmember is disqualified from taking part in the decisions regarding the project
and must recuse herself from the decisions.

2. Notwithstanding the fact that the councilmember has a disqualifying conflict of interest in
the decisions, the councilmember is permitted to appear before the city council at noticed public
meetings to represent her personal interest and is not prohibited from communications with the
general public or the press, as more fully explained below.

3. Decisions regarding the scope of any required or draft environmental report are
inextricably related to decisions regarding the project. The councilmember may not take part in the
decisions except as permitted to represent her personal interest.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Burbank and are seeking advice on behalf of
Burbank Council Member Emily Gabel-Luddy. Councilmember Gabel-Luddy resides within 500
feet of a proposed housing development.

You describe the project as the revitalization of a 7.5-acre parcel. The parcel consists of
three buildings and parking lots. The parcel is located at a major intersection of Main Street and
Riverside Drive. The parcel is currently used for event/meeting space, a 21-lane bowling alley, and
a separate ice rink. The project proposed consists of demolishing the existing structures and
building 312 residential units of townhouses and apartments with hotel-like amenities including
both active and passive outdoor spaces.

The applicant for the project will be seeking from the City a General Plan Amendment from
commercial to multifamily designation, a change in zoning, and most likely a development
agreement. A development agreement, if approved, will grant the developer vested rights to build
the project, as well as require certain improvements for the benefit of the public. The applicant is
still conducting community outreach meetings before submitting final applications for entitlements
to the City. In terms of environmental review, the City anticipates that the project will necessitate
the completion of either a Mitigated Negative Declaration or an Environmental Impact Report.
There has been no environmental analysis on the project since the application has not been
submitted, yet. The developer estimates that the application for entitlements will be submitted to the
City by the end of September.

Councilmember Gabel-Luddy has an interest in her real property, which is her personal
residence and valued at over $2,000. Both her property and the project are located within an area of
the City called the Rancho and across the street from the Los Angeles Equestrian Center. The
Rancho is zoned "residential-horse keeping" and allows stables for horses. The councilmember's
property is part of the Rancho and is a horse property. Her property is one block from the
intersection of Main and Riverside and the project parcel.

Under current zoning, Councilmember Gabel-Luddy is allowed to house up to four horses
on her property, although she does not currently board her horse at home. Horse riding is normal in

File No. A-17-208
Page No. 3

the Rancho, as riders make their way to the public trails near the Equestrian Center or to the horse
bridge leading to Griffith Park. Horses must traverse alleys and streets to access the public trails and
one of those streets is adjacent to the proposed project.

ANALYSIS

1. Is councilmember who owns a residence within 500 feet of a proposed housing project
disqualified from taking part in the decisions regarding the project?

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Different standards
apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material
depending on the nature of the interest.

Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Under
Sections 82033 and 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property owned directly,
indirectly, or beneficially by the public official, or his or her immediate family, if the interest has a
fair market value of $2,000 or more. For Councilmember Gabel-Luddy, the only interest implicated
by the decision is her property interest in her residence. Thus, we must determine whether the
financial effect on her interest in her residence resulting from decisions regarding the project is both
foreseeable and material.

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest.
(Regulation 18702.) The materiality standards for any particular interest are provided in
Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5.

Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Gabel-Luddy's real property interest is not
explicitly involved in decisions regarding the project and a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable
if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.
Moreover, any reasonably foreseeable effect on a residential interest in real property is material
whenever the governmental decision affects real property located within 500 feet of the property
line of the official's property, unless the Commission determines that there are sufficient facts to
indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the property.
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(11).)

In this case, the facts indicate that the project is proposed as the "revitalization" of a parcel
and the magnitude of the proposed housing project is of sufficient size that it is foreseeable to
expect some financial effects on properties within a close proximity of the project. Thus, there are
insufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on

File No. A-17-208
Page No. 4

Councilmember Gable-Luddy's property. The councilmember may not take part in the decisions
and must recuse herself from the decisions.²

2. If disqualified, to what extent can the councilmember comment on the project as a
member of the public at meeting attended by other city officials, at public meetings not attended by
other city officials, and with the press?

An official disqualified from a decision may not make, participate in making, or use his or
her official position to influence a decision. An official makes a decision if the official authorizes or
directs any action, votes, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. An official
participates in a decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for
the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive review. For a
decision before the official's agency, an official uses his or her official position to influence a
decision if he or she contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency
subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting the
decision. (Regulation 18704.)

However, the Regulation 18704(d) provides several exceptions to making, participating in
making, or influencing a decision. In pertinent parts, an official is not making, participating in
making, or influencing a decision if the official makes an appearance as a member of the general
public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function related to his or her
personal interests such as an interest in real property owned entirely by the official and the official's
immediate family. (Regulation 18704(d)(2).) Additionally, an official is not making, participating in
making, or influencing a decision, if the official is communicating only to the general public or the
press. (Regulation 18704(d)(4).)

Appearance to Represent a "Personal Interest" Before an Official's Own Agency

While Regulation 18704(d)(2) permits appearances before an agency to represent one's own
personal interest, this exception has been construed narrowly to apply only when an agency is
holding a noticed public meeting. Moreover, any comments must be limited to the official's
personal interests, and care should be taken to clarify that the official is not acting in an official
capacity. (McHugh Advice Letter, No. I-94-279; and Larsen Advice
Letter, No. A-87-151.) A Section 87200 filer, such as a city councilmember,
making an appearance under the exception to represent his or her personal interest, must still step
down from the dais and leave the room for the duration of the proceeding. The official may enter
the room only for the duration of time necessary to make his or her comments, and the comments
must be made in the same manner as any other member of the public.

You have specifically asked for assistance determining whether Councilmember Gabel-
Luddy may comment on issues "not strictly limited to the boundaries of her personal real estate"
such as the effects of the project on horse keeping, horse riding access and safety, and horseback

² Note that a public official (such as a city councilmember), who holds an office specified in Section 87200,
with a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting must: (1) publicly identify each
financial interest that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest, (2) recuse himself or herself from the decision,
and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.)

File No. A-17-208
Page No. 5

riding through the Rancho, as well as improvements needed at the intersection of Main Street and
Riverside. Generally. To the extent that councilmember is making a permitted appearance to
represent her personal interest, the councilmember is not prohibited from commenting on the issues
you have identified provided it is clear she is commenting because the issue may affect the value,
character, or use of the property near the project including her residence.

Communication to the General Public or Press

Pursuant to Regulation 18704(d)(4), we have previously advised that a city councilmember
may express his or her opinion to reporters and media outlets by writing a newspaper article or
appearing on a radio program regarding the matter in which he or she has a conflict-of-interest
under the Act. (Edelen Advice Letter, No. I-07-059 and Acker Advice Letter, No. A-01-117.) We
have also advised that an official is free to discuss a project with the press, friends, neighbors or
other members of the community, even if he does so in an attempt to rally support or opposition to
the project, unless they are members, officers, employees, or consultants of the city. (Mizrahi
Advice Letter, No. A-12-036.)

You have specifically asked about Councilmember Gabel-Luddy's involvement at meetings,
both private and public, regarding the project at which another councilmember or agency staff may
or may not be present. In this regard, the Act does not prohibit the councilmember from meeting
with the public or participating in public or private meeting regarding the project if there are no
other councilmembers, officers, employees, or consultants of the city in attendance. However, the
councilmember is generally prohibited from attending any private meeting or public meeting that is
also attended by another councilmember, officer, employee, or consultant of the city except to
represent a personal interest at a noticed public meeting as permitted by Regulation 18704(d)(2), as
analyzed above.

3. If disqualified from decisions regarding the project, can the councilmember comment on
the scope of any required or draft environmental impact report?

The Act's conflict of interest provisions are generally analyzed with respect to foreseeability
and materiality on a decision-by-decision basis unless the decisions are "inextricably interrelated."
Decisions are inextricably interrelated when the result of one decision will effectively determine,
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation 18706(b).) In examining when
decisions are inextricably interrelated, we have previously determined that an official disqualified
from a project is also disqualified from major policies decisions regarding a project (O'Harra
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174, citing Athan Advice Letter, No. A-86-094.) Similarly, in the
Condrashoff Advice Letter, No. I-09-030, we advised:

"[T]he funding decisions may also be interlinked with all the other decisions
because a decision to obtain (or not obtain) funding or a decision as to the funding
source, or amount of funding, would likely have an impact on the extent of the
project or project going forward. This would also apply to any environmental impact
reports that are necessary elements to the project."

In this case, comments regarding the scope of any required or draft environmental impact
report are major policy decisions that may potentially affect the feasibility of the project.

File No. A-17-208
Page No. 6

Accordingly, we find decisions regarding the scope of any required or draft environmental report are inextricably related to decisions regarding the project and Councilmember Gabel-Luddy may not take part in the decisions except as permitted to represent her personal interest under Regulation 18704(d)(2), as analyzed above.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

inextricably related to decisions regarding the project. The councilmember may not take part in the 
decisions except as permitted to represent her personal interest.

riding through the Rancho, as well as improvements needed at the intersection of Main Street and

Communication to the General Public or Press 
Pursuant to Regulation I 8704(d)(4), we have previously advised that a city councilmember

appearing on a radio program regarding the matter in which he or she has a conflict-of-interest

the project, unless they are members, officers, employees, or consultants of the city. (Mizrahi 
Advice Letter, No. A-12-036.)

other council members, officers, employees, or consultants of the city in attendance. However, the 
councilmember is generally prohibited from attending any private meeting or public meeting that is 
also attended by another councilmember, officer, employee, or consultant of the city except to

analyzed above.

the scope of any required or draft environmental impact report? 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions are generally analyzed with respect to foreseeability 
and materiality on a decision-by-decision basis unless the decisions are "inextricably interrelated." 
Decisions are inextricably interrelated when the result of one decision will effectively determine, 
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation l 8706(b).) In examining when 
decisions are inextricably interrelated, we have previously determined that an official disqualified 
from a project is also disqualified from major policies decisions regarding a project ( 0 'Harra 
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174, citing Athan Advice Letter, No. A-86-094.) Similarly, in the 
Co11dras/10_ff Advice Letter, No. 1-09-030, we advised: 
"[T]he funding decisions may also be interlinked with all the other decisions 
because a decision to obtain ( or not obtain) funding or a decision as to the funding 
source, or amount of funding, would likely have an impact on the extent of the 
project or project going forward. This would also apply to any environmental impact 
reports that are necessary elements to the project." 
In this case, comments regarding the scope of any required or draft environmental impact 
report are major policy decisions that may potentially affect the feasibility of the project.  
Accordingly, we find decisions regarding the scope of any required or draft environmental report 
are inextricably related to decisions regarding the project and Councilmember Gabel-Luddy may 
not take part in the decisions except as permitted to represent her personal interest under Regulation 
18704(d)(2), as analyzed above.

disqualified from taking part in the decisions regarding the project? 
2. If disqualified, to what extent can the councilmember comment on the project as a 
member of the public at meeting attended by other city officials, at public meetings not attended by 
other city officials, and with the press? 
3. If disqualified from decisions regarding the project, can the councilmember comment on 
the scope of any required or draft environmental impact report?

CONCLUSION: I. The councilmember is disqualified from taking part in the decisions regarding the project 
and must recuse herself from the decisions. 
2. Notwithstanding the fact that the councilmember has a disqualifying conflict of interest in 
the decisions, the councilmember is permitted to appear before the city council at noticed public 
meetings to represent her personal interest and is not prohibitc.-d from communications with the 
general public or the press, as more fully explained below. 
3. Decisions regarding the scope of any required or draft environmental report are 
inextricably related to decisions regarding the project. The councilmember may not take part in the 
decisions except as permitted to represent her personal interest.
